title: Hidden library prefixes preserve provenance through color
summary: When a common library prefix is removed from visible text, the remaining identifier or type keeps that library's category color.
details: CUDA complex types use their accepted cf32/cf64 spellings in CUDA seafoam, including reference, pointer, template, expression, and moved-return positions.
A visible const qualifier is gray and the following prefix-stripped library type retains its provenance color.
Vk/VK_/vk, GLFW/glfw, ImGui/Im/IM_, CUDA driver/runtime, VMA, OpenGL, and other configured families follow the same classify-before-strip principle where their prefix is hidden.
Color is not decoration trivia in this visual language: it carries the origin information removed from text.
rationale: Removing both the textual prefix and its category color would erase useful provenance and make unrelated short names indistinguishable.
Classifying the original token before stripping is deterministic and works for overlay and raw-line renderers.
alternatives: Hide prefixes and color every resulting type as a generic blue; rejected because the hidden provenance is lost.
Retain every prefix textually; remains an open per-library choice for families whose prefixes are not currently hidden.

provenance.note: Owner stated that hiding a prefix requires retaining the library color.
Classification uses the unstripped underlying type; visible qualifiers are split into their own gray chunks.

evidence.claim: CUDA concrete references and const pointers keep DansCUDA after the cu prefix is removed.
The browser consumes actual Neovim RGB spans rather than reconstructing category color in CSS.

questions: Why is cf32 green after cuFloatComplex is compacted?
What information replaces a concealed library prefix?
Should visible const share the library type color?

last_verified.method: Owner review followed by chunk-level assertions and RGB ext-linegrid inspection.
